title: Psalms 104
subtitle: The Creation Hymn

heb.paragraph: The psalm opens with the imperative בָּרֲכִי נַפְשִׁי (bārăkhî napšî) — 'Bless, O my soul!' This reflexive call to praise brackets the psalm (vv.1, 35), framing the entire creation hymn as an act of worship. The verb בָּרַךְ (bārakh) means to kneel in homage, to bless. The psalmist summons his own נֶפֶשׁ (nepeš) — his whole animated self — to the task of praising the Creator.

"He wraps himself in light (אוֹר) as with a garment" (v.2). Light is God's clothing — not metaphor for mere brightness but for unapproachable glory (cf. 1 Tim 6:16). The creation account begins with light (Gen 1:3); here the Creator himself is robed in it. The image suggests both radiance and hiddenness: light reveals, yet staring at the sun blinds.

"He stretches out (נוֹטֶה) the heavens like a tent" (v.2). The participle נוֹטֶה (from נָטָה, nāṭâ) depicts ongoing action — God continually holds the sky in place like tent fabric. The same verb describes pitching a nomad's tent (Gen 12:8). The cosmos is God's dwelling, casually erected like a Bedouin shelter. What fills humans with awe is, for the Creator, routine habitation.

"You covered it with the deep (תְּהוֹם) as with a garment" (v.6). תְּהוֹם echoes Genesis 1:2 — the primordial waters over which the Spirit hovered. In ANE mythology, the deep (Tiamat in Babylonian) was a chaos-monster to be defeated. Here it is merely a garment God drapes and then pushes back at His rebuke (v.7). No battle, just command. The waters that could swallow the earth now obey boundaries (v.9).

"You set a boundary (גְּבוּל) they cannot cross" (v.9). The noun גְּבוּל means border, limit, territory. God's word establishes cosmic zoning: the sea has its place, the land has its place. This is covenant language applied to creation — YHWH sets limits that chaos cannot transgress. The same word describes Israel's territorial boundaries (Num 34). Creation order mirrors covenant order.

"He set the earth on its foundations (יָסַד)" (v.5). The verb יָסַד means to found, establish, lay a base. The earth is not floating randomly but anchored on מְכוֹנֶיהָ (mĕkhônêhā) — its 'bases' or 'foundations.' This is architectural language: God is a master builder who constructs the world to stand firm. "It can never be moved" (בַּל־תִּמּוֹט) — creation is stable because God made it so.

notes.note: "Praise the LORD, my soul. LORD my God, you are very great; you are clothed with splendour and majesty. The LORD wraps himself in light as with a garment; he stretches out the heavens like a tent." Light is God’s clothing. The heavens are his tent. Creation is God’s wardrobe and architecture.

"He set the earth on its foundations; it can never be moved. You covered it with the watery depths. At your rebuke the waters fled. You set a boundary they cannot cross." Order from chaos: boundaries set, waters controlled, land established.

"He makes grass grow for the cattle, and plants for people to cultivate — bringing forth food from the earth: wine that gladdens human hearts, oil to make their faces shine, and bread that sustains their hearts." The theology of daily provision: wine (joy), oil (beauty), bread (strength). God provides delight, not just survival.

ane.parallel: Great Hymn to Aten (Egypt, c. 14th cent. BCE)
ane.similarity: Both texts praise a solar-associated deity for creating and sustaining all life. Both describe animals depending on the light for activity and retreating when darkness falls. Both celebrate the deity providing water for plants and creatures. The sequence of images — sun rising, animals stirring, humans working, then resting at sunset — appears in both. One of the most discussed parallels in ANE studies.
ane.difference: The Aten hymn praises the sun disk itself as divine; Psalm 104 subordinates the sun to Yahweh who created it. The Egyptian text emerged from Akhenaten's monotheistic revolution and disappeared with his reign. The psalm integrates creation praise into Israel's ongoing worship of the covenant God.
ane.significance: The parallel raises questions about possible Egyptian influence on Israelite poetry. Whether direct borrowing or independent development, both texts show ANE poets celebrating creation order. The psalm transforms solar imagery into praise of the transcendent Creator.

hist.context: Psalm 104 is the Hebrew Bible's great creation hymn, widely compared to the Egyptian Hymn to the Aten attributed to Pharaoh Akhenaten (14th century BCE). The parallels — the sun's life-giving role, animals seeking food from the deity, the comprehensive scope of creation — are striking enough that many scholars posit a literary relationship, though the theological framework differs radically: Aten is the sun itself, while YHWH is the creator who uses light as his garment (v.2). The psalm follows the creation sequence of Genesis 1 loosely but not exactly, suggesting both draw on a common Israelite creation theology rather than one depending directly on the other.

refs.note: The psalm follows the creation week's sequence: light, waters, land, vegetation, luminaries, creatures.
God's speech from the whirlwind cataloguing creation parallels this psalm's survey.
All things created through Christ and held together in him fulfills the psalm's creation theology.

sections.header: Verses 19–35 — “How Many Are Your Works, LORD!”

heb.paragraph: "There is the leviathan (liwyāṭān), which you formed to frolic there" (v.26). The moon marks seasons, the sun knows when to set (v.19). Darkness brings night-creatures; dawn sends them back and humans go to work (vv.20–23). "How many are your works, LORD! In wisdom you made them all" (v.24). The sea teems with creatures; Leviathan frolics in it (v.26). "All creatures look to you to give them their food at the proper time" (v.27). God’s breath gives life; withdrawing it brings death; sending it forth renews creation (vv.29–30). The psalm closes: "May my meditation be pleasing to him, as I rejoice in the LORD" (v.34).

notes.note: "How many are your works, LORD! In wisdom you made them all; the earth is full of your creatures." The exclamation at creation’s abundance: too many to count, all made wisely, filling the earth.
"There is the leviathan, which you formed to frolic there." Leviathan — the chaos-monster of Job 41 — is God’s pet that plays in the sea. Terror becomes toy. What Job feared, the psalmist celebrates.

"When you hide your face, they are terrified; when you take away their breath, they die. When you send your Spirit, they are created, and you renew the face of the ground." The Spirit as life-giver: breath withdrawn = death; breath sent = new creation. The cycle of life depends entirely on God’s Spirit.
"Praise the LORD, my soul. Praise the LORD." The psalm ends as it began: personal praise. Hallelujah — the first occurrence of this word in the Psalter.

refs.note: God giving all life and breath and everything parallels the psalm's life-breath theology.
God feeding birds and clothing grass develops the psalm's providential care.
Creation groaning anticipates the psalm's vision of creation dependent on God's favor.

hist.context: The declaration 'when you take away their breath, they die' (v.29) acknowledges all life depends on God's sustaining will. Leviathan 'frolicking' in the sea (v.26) transforms a chaos monster into a playful creature — God's sovereignty is so complete that even sea dragons become pets. The closing wish for sinners to vanish jars against the psalm's joy but reflects wisdom tradition's moral seriousness.

rows.label: Creation Sustained — 104:19–35
rows.text: "How MANY are your works!" Leviathan frolics. Breath gives life. "PRAISE THE LORD." First hallelujah.

hebtext.note: "There is the leviathan (liwyāṭān), which you formed to frolic there" (v.26). The moon marks seasons, the sun knows when to set (v.19). Darkness brings night-creatures; dawn sends them back and humans go to work (vv.20–23). "How many are your works, LORD! In wisdom you made them all" (v.24). The sea teems with creatures; Leviathan frolics in it (v.26). "All creatures look to you to give them their food at the proper time" (v.27). God’s breath gives life; withdrawing it brings death; sending it forth renews creation (vv.29–30). The psalm closes: "May my meditation be pleasing to him, as I rejoice in the LORD" (v.34).

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10: the creation hymn — God orders, sustains, and renews all life. Worship at 9. First hallelujah. Leviathan frolics.

prayer_prompt: The creation hymn celebrates God's sustaining care for all creatures. Consider asking God about your place in His creation — how you receive His care and extend it to others.
